fr.elements: Main Building; Roof System; Landscaped Area; Patio Area
fr.recommendations: Enhancing emergency response effectiveness on the property could begin with ensuring unobstructed access by addressing the limited side access and dense vegetation. Trimming back or strategically thinning out dense vegetation may assist in improving responder visibility and movement, allowing for more efficient navigation and staging around the property. It would also allow responders to identify and access critical areas more quickly. Maintaining clear outer perimeters, particularly around the main building and patio area, could facilitate responder access and operational space. Implementing these adjustments may enhance overall emergency operations by enabling better visibility and streamlined movement for both personnel and equipment across the property.

wildfire.conditions: Tree Cluster; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Scattered Trees; Dense Tree Canopy; Vegetated Perimeter; Limited Defensible Space; Vegetation Adjacent to Fence; Open Terrain
wildfire.recommendations: Enhancing defensible space could be achieved by increasing the distance between the structure and the surrounding vegetation, especially where vegetation is adjacent to the structure and fence. Thinning the tree cluster and managing the dense tree canopy to reduce fuel continuity and separation from the home could further minimize potential fire exposure. Addressing the vegetative perimeter by creating breaks or maintaining open terrain may limit opportunities for fire spread towards the property. Consideration should also be given to maintaining or improving open areas and scattered trees to enhance firefighter access and support suppression operations. Ongoing management of vegetative growth around the property perimeter and adjacent structures will be crucial in supporting long-term wildfire resilience and ensuring preparedness.
